items.title: US–Iran MoU signed: oil falls, small caps +400bps, peace dividend already spent
items.idea: A Memorandum of Understanding between the US and Iran was reached late in the week, replacing the threat of resumed war with a fragile diplomatic framework. The immediate market reaction: oil prices tumbled (Costco regular gas at $3.83/gal), gold got relief, dollar pulled back, and small caps surged +400bps on the week vs SPX +60bps. UMich sentiment bounced off a record low. Bond Beat's bottom line: 'The market has already spent the peace dividend, priced the rate cuts, and bought the dip — now it just needs reality to cooperate.'
items.angle: The asymmetry here is interesting: the market celebrated the MoU as if it resolved the structural Hormuz risk, but Goldman's oil team extended their export-normalization timeline anyway (to late August). The 'peace dividend' is priced; the supply uncertainty is not.
items.tweetA: The market celebrated the US-Iran MoU by sending small caps up 400bps. Oil fell. Gas at $3.83 at Costco. Everyone exhaled. Bond Beat's line is the right one: 'The market has already spent the peace dividend.' Now it needs reality to cooperate.
items.tweetB: US-Iran MoU → oil down, IWM up 400bps, gas cheaper. Goldman STILL extended Hormuz normalization to late August. The market priced ceasefire. The barrel problem isn't solved. Watch the gap between sentiment and supply.

items.title: ERP hits +0.39% — Treasury out-paying S&P earnings for first time since 2003
items.idea: The US 10-year yield minus the S&P 500's trailing earnings yield hit +0.39% — meaning a risk-free Treasury literally pays more than owning the 500 greatest companies in America. This is the highest reading since 2003, after touching +0.53% earlier. Last time this flipped positive going into a sustained period: 1999 rolling into 2000. After the March 2000 peak the S&P lost ~50% over 2.5 years. The Nasdaq fell -78% from 5,048 to 1,114 — and then made buyers wait until April 2015 merely to break even. TINA (There Is No Alternative) is mathematically reversed.
items.angle: This is THE macro chart of 2026. Stocks aren't priced for a world where the risk-free rate beats them. Either earnings accelerate dramatically from here, or capital begins drifting. In 20 years Macro Mornings says he's never found a third door.
items.tweetA: The 10-year Treasury now pays you MORE than owning the S&P 500. ERP at +0.39%. Last time this happened was 1999-2000. What followed: S&P -50%, Nasdaq -78%, 15 years to break even. Either earnings outrun everything forever, or capital migrates. No third option.
items.tweetB: TINA is dead. The earnings yield on the S&P 500 is now below the risk-free rate. You're paying a penalty to own equities over bonds. The market hasn't repriced this yet. But markets never do — until they do, violently.

charts.desc: 80-year ERP chart showing the +0.39% reading vs dot-com, plus 11-month SuperCore PPI and 9-chart constellation. Worth opening the full article.

items.title: SuperCore PPI 11 straight months of gains, May PPI 6.5% — back to pandemic-era pipeline inflation
items.idea: SuperCore PPI (stripping food, energy, and trade — the cleanest read on pipeline inflation) has climbed for 11 consecutive months, reaching 5.1%. May PPI headline printed 6.5% (beat 6.4% estimate), highest since November 2022. Core PPI 4.9%. May CPI 4.2%, fastest pace in 3 years. The last time this gauge ran this hot, the Fed responded with 525bp of tightening in 16 months. The S&P shed -19.4% in 2022, Nasdaq -33%, worst year since 2008. The pipeline is full.
items.angle: Eleven consecutive months of SuperCore PPI gains isn't a wobble, it's a structural trend. The Fed meeting this week under Warsh is critical — if he drops forward guidance AND the dots show a hold, this inflation data makes 2027 cuts look optimistic.
items.tweetA: SuperCore PPI: 11 straight months of gains. Now at 5.1%. May CPI: 4.2% — fastest in 3 years. May PPI: 6.5%, highest since Nov 2022. We're back at pandemic-stimulus pipeline inflation. The last time this happened the Fed raised 525bps. Markets are not pricing that.
items.tweetB: Eleven candles on the inflation cake. SuperCore PPI hasn't paused in almost a year. Core PPI 4.9%. CPI 4.2%. And we're about to have the first Warsh FOMC — where the dot plot will reportedly show NO cuts this year. The 'soft landing' narrative has a math problem.

charts.desc: SuperCore PPI 11-month trend chart + May CPI/PPI comparison vs 2022. Highly visual, tweet-worthy.

items.title: First Kevin Warsh FOMC this Wednesday — Barclays: no easing bias, hold 2026, 25bp cut 2027
items.idea: This Wednesday is Kevin Warsh's first FOMC meeting as Fed Chair. Barclays expects the Committee to hold rates unchanged, drop any reference to forward guidance (eliminating the easing bias), and project NO cuts in 2026 via the dot plot — just a single 25bp cut in 2027 and a hold in 2028. Bespoke's historical analysis shows stocks move LOWER on new Fed chair's first meeting day. Retail Sales data hits the same morning.
items.angle: The market has priced optimism around Warsh-as-dove, but the data (PPI 6.5%, CPI 4.2%, SuperCore PPI 11-month climb) makes easing nearly impossible to justify. If he drops the easing bias entirely, the repricing in bonds and equities could be sharp.
items.tweetA: Warsh's first FOMC is Wednesday. Barclays: no easing bias, no cuts in 2026, one cut in 2027. Bespoke historical: stocks fall on new Fed chair's first meeting. May PPI was 6.5%. What exactly is the bull case for a pivot here?
items.tweetB: Kevin Warsh chairs his first Fed meeting in 4 days. The market priced him as a potential dove. The inflation data says 6.5% PPI, 4.2% CPI, 11 months of rising SuperCore PPI. Barclays expects him to DROP the easing bias entirely. This could move.

charts.desc: Bespoke chart on stock performance on new Fed chair first meeting days. Worth opening.

items.source: Dark Side of the Boom / Stephen Innes (Goldman GS analysis)
items.title: Goldman: 14-15mb/d Hormuz shock only became 5-6mb/d deficit — demand destruction absorbed 60%
items.idea: Goldman Sachs's Daan Struyven team: a 14-15mb/d Middle East production hit translated into only a 5-6mb/d Q2 deficit because demand destruction, pre-war oversupply, and stronger ex-Mideast supply absorbed the majority of the shock. Gulf exports can normalize if Hormuz flows return to roughly 70% of pre-war levels via rerouting through Yanbu, Fujairah, Gulf of Oman, and Ceyhan. Goldman maintains Q4 2026 Brent at $90.
items.angle: Oil has stopped trading like a one-way geopolitical squeeze and started trading like a messy balance-sheet problem. 60% of a 14-15mb/d shock was absorbed by demand destruction alone. That changes the volatility structure of the oil trade.
items.tweetA: Goldman's oil call: 14-15mb/d Middle East production shock → only 5-6mb/d actual deficit. Demand destruction absorbed 60% of the blow before it even hit the market. Brent Q4 still $90 but 2027 cut to $80. Oil isn't a one-way Hormuz trade anymore. It's a balance sheet problem.
items.tweetB: The surprising thing about the Hormuz shock isn't the disruption — it's how little of it moved the needle on actual supply balances. Goldman: demand destruction + pre-war oversupply + rerouting ate most of the hit. If Hormuz opens to 70% of normal, Q4 Brent stays at $90.

charts.desc: Charts inside the full article — worth opening

items.source: Dark Side of the Boom / Stephen Innes (Goldman GS analysis)
items.title: Goldman 2027 Brent cut to $80 on projected 3.5mb/d surplus — security premium holds the floor
items.idea: Goldman's team cuts their 2027 average Brent forecast by $5 to $80, projecting a 3.5mb/d surplus as the balance sheet normalizes post-disruption. The normalization timeline was extended to late August (from late June). Despite the heavy 2027 balance, low OECD commercial stocks, SPR rebuilding programs, and a lingering security premium prevent Brent from collapsing into pure surplus pricing.
items.angle: The 3.5mb/d 2027 surplus is the number to watch: if Iranian production comes back faster than expected, that surplus grows. The security premium is the only floor — and it's not mechanical.
items.tweetA: Goldman cut 2027 Brent $5 to $80, projecting a 3.5mb/d surplus. SPR rebuilding + security premium keep it from collapsing. But: if Iranian production ramps faster than modeled or demand destruction proves structural, that surplus number gets much bigger. $80 could be the ceiling, not the floor.
items.tweetB: The 2027 oil balance: 3.5mb/d surplus in Goldman's base case. The question isn't whether supply recovers — it will. It's whether demand recovers with it. Goldman's note quietly acknowledges demand destruction may not fully snap back. That's the part the market isn't pricing.

items.source: Bubble Bubble Report / Jesse Colombo + Robert Sinn
items.title: Gold holding $3,900–$4,100 support; silver double-bottom at $60–$70; first Warsh FOMC is the catalyst
items.idea: Gold fell sharply early in the week before finding support at the $3,900–$4,100 zone. Needs to reclaim $4,300–$4,600 for a sign of strength. Silver staged a solid bounce from the $60–$70 support zone. Jesse Colombo: Iran war developments and this week's first Warsh FOMC are the key catalysts. Gold bounced when Trump called off plans to escalate Iran attacks.
items.angle: Gold at $3,900-4,100 with a Warsh FOMC and 4.2% CPI = an interesting setup. If Warsh drops the easing bias, the 'no cuts = dollar strength = gold weaker' narrative could re-assert. But CPI at 4.2% with supply chain risks tells a different story.
items.tweetA: Gold held exactly at $3,900-$4,100 support this week. Silver bounced from $60-$70. The precious metals complex survived the Hormuz-to-MoU whipsaw. Now the test is Warsh's first FOMC Wednesday. 4.2% CPI vs. a potential hawkish surprise. The setup is tighter than it looks.
items.tweetB: Silver crashed 50% from $121 to below $70. ETF holders reduced exposure by 74 million ounces. Speculators left. And now a double-bottom is forming. This is what capitulation looks like. The Warsh FOMC is the near-term binary — but structurally, the panic selling is already done.

charts.desc: Charts inside both sources — key support/resistance zones for gold and silver. Worth opening.

items.title: Silver -50% from January $121 peak: double-bottom forming with 74M oz ETF outflow as capitulation signal
items.idea: Silver peaked at $121/oz during a speculative frenzy in January, then fell ~50% to below $70/oz. Silver ETF holdings declined by 74 million ounces since the start of the year, indicating significant speculative deleveraging. Options open interest and futures positioning also reduced. Last week silver reversed higher and a potential double-bottom formed. Silver growth stocks showing deep value.
items.angle: 74 million ounces of ETF outflows = the tourist money has left. The structural buyers (industrial demand for solar, EVs, grid) haven't gone anywhere. If the double-bottom holds, this is a classic re-accumulation setup after forced selling clears.
items.tweetA: Silver went from $121 to below $70. ETF holders sold 74 million ounces. Options cleared. Open interest collapsed. And now a double-bottom is forming. The speculative froth is gone. What's left is the industrial demand story — solar, EVs, grid storage — that was always underneath.
items.tweetB: I keep watching silver. -50% from peak, 74M oz of ETF selling, futures deleveraging. That's not a market being rationally repriced. That's a market being liquidated. When the selling exhausts itself and a double-bottom forms, that's usually worth paying attention to. $SLV

charts.desc: Monthly silver chart showing the blow-off top and potential double-bottom. Charts inside — worth opening.

items.title: SpaceX IPO +19% day one — ARK: launch cost fell 95% since 2008, $30B/GW from AI vs $15B from compute rental
items.idea: SpaceX (SPCX) IPO'd and surged 19% on Friday. ARK's thesis: launch costs fell ~95% since 2008 ($15,600/kg → <$1,000/kg). Starlink has 12M+ active customers targeting a $1.6T market. Vertical integration with xAI could allow SpaceX to capture $30B+ per GW per year from AI model output vs ~$15B/GW from compute leasing. Existing compute leasing agreements with Anthropic and Google generating near-term revenue. ARK frames SpaceX as converged AI/robotics/energy storage infrastructure.
items.angle: The doubling of value-capture from $15B to $30B/GW hinges on one bet: that the model layer (xAI) extracts more economics than the infrastructure layer. SpaceX is making this same AI-stack debate in orbit.
items.tweetA: SpaceX IPO'd +19% day one. ARK's thesis: vertical integration with xAI yields $30B/GW from model output vs $15B/GW from compute rental. The Starlink story was always bigger than 'satellite internet.' It's orbital AI infrastructure monetized through the model layer.
items.tweetB: SpaceX IPO: 12M Starlink customers. $1.6T addressable market. Launch costs down 95% from 2008. And Anthropic + Google already leasing compute from them. The question isn't whether SpaceX is valuable — it's whether the public is getting in after the best returns are already priced.

items.title: ROKU +20% on M&A rumor; Cerebras +18% on Wall Street initiations; LUNR +15% on SpaceX IPO halo
items.idea: Three ARK names moved 15%+ this week: Roku (ROKU) surged 20% Friday on reports of acquisition discussions with at least one US media company. Cerebras Systems (CRBS) rose 18% Monday after Morgan Stanley, UBS, Needham, and Mizuho all simultaneously initiated with Buy/Outperform/Overweight — citing technical differentiation and OpenAI commercial traction. Intuitive Machines (LUNR) rose 15% Thursday as space stocks broadly re-rated ahead of the SpaceX IPO.
items.angle: Cerebras is interesting: simultaneous initiations from multiple banks on the same day often signals a coordinated lock-up expiry window or a deal process. The OpenAI traction story is the hook, but the timing of the analyst pile-on is worth noting.
items.tweetA: ROKU +20% on M&A talk. CRBS +18% as Morgan Stanley, UBS, Needham, Mizuho all initiated simultaneously. LUNR +15% on SpaceX IPO halo. Three names. Three different catalysts. One week. The space/AI/media convergence trade is not subtle.
items.tweetB: Cerebras Systems +18% on Wall Street initiations. Multiple banks. Same day. All Buy-equivalent. The thesis: technical differentiation + OpenAI traction. Worth asking: why did MS, UBS, Needham, and Mizuho all pull the trigger on the same Monday? That coordination usually means something.

items.title: Claude Fable 5 released: Stripe compressed 50M-line migration from 2 months to 1 day; $10/$50 per M tokens
items.idea: Anthropic released Claude Fable 5 (Mythos-class), the most capable model widely available. SOTA on Cognition's FrontierCode. Stripe reported it compressed a 50-million-line codebase migration from two months of human work into a single day. Holds focus across millions of tokens. 3x better on strategic gameplay with persistent memory. Pricing: $10/M input, $50/M output. Rollout continues through June 22. A restricted 'Claude Mythos 5' remains limited to Project Glasswing partners.
items.angle: The Stripe number is the one that matters: 50M lines, 2 months → 1 day. That's not a benchmark — that's enterprise ROI that CFOs can compute. This is the moment AI coding crosses from 'productivity tool' to 'structural headcount arbitrage.'
items.tweetA: Stripe used Claude Fable 5 to migrate a 50-million-line codebase. It took 1 day. It would have taken human engineers 2 months. That's not a benchmark. That's a CFO conversation about engineering headcount. $10/M in, $50/M out. The math is not subtle.
items.tweetB: Claude Fable 5: SOTA on FrontierCode, millions of tokens of context, 3x better on strategic reasoning. And then there's the Stripe case study: 50M lines, 2 months compressed to a day. The gap between frontier AI capability and what most enterprises have deployed is still enormous.

items.title: Apple rebuilds Siri on Google Gemini — the $3T company decided not to build a frontier model
items.idea: Apple is rebuilding Siri on Google Gemini rather than competing at the frontier model layer. If Apple — with $3 trillion market cap, world-class ML talent, and decades of on-device AI — concluded it wasn't worth building a frontier LLM, the competitive dynamics at the frontier become even more concentrated. Also this week: Kimi K2.7-Code open-sources a 1 trillion parameter coding model, NotebookLM goes agentic, Claude Code adds nested subagents.
items.angle: Apple's choice to embed Gemini is a massive validation for Google's distribution strategy. Apple used to negotiate leverage by offering to embed Google Search. Now they're doing it for AI. This is a repeat of the same power dynamic — and Google just won.
items.tweetA: Apple rebuilt Siri on Google Gemini. The most valuable company in the world looked at building a frontier AI model and said no. Google just won the Apple distribution deal twice — once for search, once for AI. The frontier is more concentrated, not less.
items.tweetB: Kimi K2.7-Code: 1 trillion parameter open-source coder. NotebookLM goes agentic. Claude Code adds nested subagents. Apple ships Gemini on every iPhone. This is a single week. The AI capability stack is moving faster than any deployment roadmap can track.

items.title: The 'Own Era' thesis: AI is the height of Read/Write, not a break — the scramble to own it is the clearest proof
items.idea: Robert Hackett (a16z crypto) deploys Chris Dixon's framework: internet evolved through Read, Write, and now Own eras. Key insight: AI is NOT a break from the internet's trajectory. It is the logical conclusion of the Read/Write era — 30 years of driving down the cost of reading, writing, and manipulating information, consummated at last in software. The scramble to own AI (SPVs, secondaries, pre-IPO perpetual futures) is pent-up demand for the Own era breaking loose. People don't just want to use AI — they want to own it.
items.angle: This reframes the entire AI investment frenzy. The synthetic ownership instruments aren't irrational — they're the natural expression of a generation that learned to expect ownership of the platforms they use. Crypto native logic applied to AI.
items.tweetA: A16z's Robert Hackett: AI isn't the next internet. It's the peak of the last one — 30 years of Read/Write consummated in software. The SPVs, secondaries, and pre-IPO futures aren't a bubble. They're the Own era finally breaking loose. People want to own what they use.
items.tweetB: The scramble to own AI through SPVs and pre-IPO derivatives is routing around every structural barrier. A16z's read: this is pent-up demand for the 'own era' — the same force that created crypto — now aimed at AI companies. The demand is rational. The vehicles are experimental.

items.title: CoreWeave/Jane Street: $1B purchase then dump — the dot-com incentive asymmetry playbook
items.idea: Herb Greenberg's CoreWeave/Jane Street analysis: Jane Street bought $1B in CRWV stock as part of a $6B cloud services agreement, then reportedly sold more than they bought while allowing Magnetar to exit. ChatGPT's assessment: 'If you stripped away the names CoreWeave and Jane Street and replaced them with CMGI, JDS Uniphase... Herb's voice would be instantly recognizable.' The pattern: public arrives at the party just as the hosts are leaving. Information asymmetry between insiders and retail.
items.angle: The structural issue is classic: a large institutional counterparty in a commercial deal also holds the equity. The cloud deal creates a price-supporting narrative while the equity gets distributed. The tell is always: who's selling and what do they know?
items.tweetA: Jane Street buys $1B in CoreWeave as part of a $6B cloud deal. Then reportedly sells. Lets Magnetar exit too. Herb Greenberg's take: 'I keep coming back to the incentives.' ChatGPT read his note and said it sounded like JDS Uniphase in 1999. Draw your own conclusions about $CRWV.
items.tweetB: The question Herb always asks about hot AI names: who's selling? CoreWeave was the AI darling. Jane Street was the cloud partner buying equity. Then they were also the seller. The story hasn't changed since the dot-com era. Only the names have.

items.title: Miners generating extraordinary margins despite pullback — trading as if economics deteriorated (they haven't)
items.idea: Tavi Costa: even after the recent pullback in metal prices, miners continue to generate margins that would have seemed extraordinary just a few years ago. Yet many are trading as if the economics of the business have materially deteriorated. Best opportunities emerge when prices decline while underlying businesses continue to perform. The market is confusing price action with fundamentals.
items.angle: If miners are generating strong cash flows at current depressed metal prices, the margin of safety at current equity prices is higher than before the pullback. Classic re-accumulation setup after forced selling clears.
items.tweetA: Miners are generating margins that seemed impossible 3 years ago. Metal prices pulled back. Mining stocks followed. But the underlying businesses? Cash flows haven't deteriorated. The market is pricing the stocks like the economics broke. They didn't. That's the trade.
items.tweetB: I keep coming back to Tavi Costa's note on mining margins. The companies are generating cash at current metal prices. The stocks have repriced as if they aren't. The market is using price action as a proxy for fundamentals. That's usually where the mispricing lives.

charts.desc: Key mining cash flow charts showing margin persistence despite metal price pullback. Subscriber only but worth seeing.

items.title: 30yr holding 5.125% support; Barclays expects no easing bias, dots show hold 2026 + 25bp cut 2027
items.idea: Bond Beat's technical view: 30-year yield finding support at 5.125% with resistance at 4.75/80. Momentum still looks more bullish than not on the weekly timeframe. Barclays FOMC preview: expects hold, drop any reference to forward guidance (eliminating easing bias), project a hold in 2026 via the dot plot, a single 25bp cut in 2027, and a hold in 2028. PIMCO: bonds can still provide portfolio diversification; CBs have more room to cut in future recessions.
items.angle: The Barclays call is significant: eliminating the easing bias entirely is more hawkish than markets are pricing. If Warsh does this in his first meeting, the long end could reprice sharply. 30yr at 5.125% support is the level to watch.
items.tweetA: Barclays FOMC preview: no easing bias, no cuts in 2026, just 25bp in 2027. If Warsh delivers this Wednesday, the bond market has to reprice. 30yr sitting at 5.125% support. At 5.03% handle last week. Watch what happens when a new Fed chair signals hawkishness with 6.5% PPI.
items.tweetB: Bond Beat's rates call: 30yr momentum still bullish, 5.125% holding as support. PIMCO says bonds aren't dead. Barclays says the Fed drops all forward guidance Wednesday. If you're bearish duration here, you're betting that a new Fed chair goes hawkish into 4.2% CPI. That's not nothing.

charts.desc: 30-year weekly yield chart showing support/resistance levels. Worth opening.

items.title: Bespoke historical: stocks fall on new Fed chair's first meeting — pattern active this Wednesday
items.idea: Bond Beat highlighted Bespoke's historical analysis showing that stocks have historically moved LOWER on the first FOMC meeting chaired by a new Federal Reserve Chair. With Kevin Warsh chairing his first meeting this Wednesday, this historical pattern is directly active. Combined with: PPI at 6.5%, CPI at 4.2%, Barclays expecting no easing bias, and the ERP sitting at the most extreme level since 2003. This is a confluence of signals, not a single catalyst.
items.angle: Historical patterns alone are weak signals. But when the historical pattern aligns with the macro setup (hawkish inflation data + new chair likely to signal hawkishness + ERP at extreme), the probability-weighted outcome shifts.
items.tweetA: Bespoke historical: stocks go DOWN on new Fed chair's first meeting day. Warsh's first is Wednesday. May PPI was 6.5%. ERP at +0.39% (most extreme since 2003). Barclays expects no easing bias. Three independent signals pointing the same direction in one week.
items.tweetB: This Wednesday: new Fed chair's first meeting. Bespoke data shows stocks historically fall. Retail Sales same morning. Dot plot likely shows no 2026 cuts. And the ERP is at a 23-year extreme. I'm not saying it happens. I'm saying the setup is unusually stacked.

charts.desc: Bespoke chart on stock returns on new Fed chair first meeting days. Worth opening.

items.title: Small caps +400bps vs SPX +60bps; Growth worst factor, Momentum best — unusual week-end divergence
items.idea: On the week: Small Caps surged just over 400bps while the S&P 500 managed only ~60bps. Factor performance was counter-intuitive: despite starting the week with a momentum unwind, Momentum ended up as the best-performing factor by week close. Small Cap Value was second. Growth was by far the worst factor for the second consecutive week — consistent with a rotation from growth to value. Eliant track record since June 2023: 191.96% vs QQQ 105.04%, SPX 78.02%.
items.angle: Two weeks running of Growth underperforming Value and Small Caps. If the ERP argument holds (bonds now pay more than equities), the natural beneficiaries are cyclicals, value, and small caps priced on near-term earnings rather than 20-year DCF.
items.tweetA: Small caps beat the S&P by 340bps in a single week. Growth was the worst factor for the second week running. Momentum somehow ended up best. The Iran MoU did more for IWM than any Fed communication this year. And the rotation from Growth to Value just keeps going.
items.tweetB: Two consecutive weeks of Growth underperforming Value and Small Caps. If the ERP is at +0.39% — a 23-year extreme — and bonds genuinely beat stocks on a risk-adjusted basis, the crowded-growth, high-multiple trade is the one that rebalances. We're watching it happen in real time.

items.title: From Tokenmaxxing to Tokenoptimizing: enterprises shifting from frontier models to open models (70-90% cheaper)
items.idea: 0xJeff's report from SuperAI 2026: clear shift from 'tokenmaxxing' (burning money on frontier models) to 'tokenoptimizing' (open models at 70-90% lower cost with 70-90% the intelligence). Major enterprises implementing company-wide policies limiting frontier model use. Engineers most excited about ClickHouse (AI observability and analytics) and Temporal (durable AI execution — workflows that survive failures). The infrastructure/observability layer is becoming the margin-capture layer.
items.angle: The same pattern that played out in cloud: everyone used premium services until cost structure forced optimization. Open models at 70-90% quality and 10-30% of the cost means the infrastructure + observability stack is the next enterprise AI spend wave.
items.tweetA: At SuperAI 2026, engineers aren't excited about the latest GPT or Claude. They're excited about ClickHouse (AI observability) and Temporal (durable agent execution). The enterprise shift from Tokenmaxxing to Tokenoptimizing is real — and it means the infrastructure layer captures the margin.
items.tweetB: 70-90% of frontier intelligence at 10-30% of the cost. That's open models today. Enterprises have noticed. Company-wide policies limiting frontier model usage are already being set. The winners of the next enterprise AI wave won't be model labs. They'll be observability, orchestration, and workflow infrastructure.

items.title: Rise of solopreneurs: non-technical founders building AI products with Claude/Codex — Balaji's Network School as the pipeline
items.idea: At SuperAI 2026, wave of solopreneurs and 2-3 person teams with no technical background building MVPs and raising pre-seed rounds. Several from Balaji's Network School where they 'vibe-code' their ideas. Pattern: start with Claude/Codex → optimize to open models as they scale. Driving factors: AI models smart enough that an idea and intention are sufficient to start building. Global job market pressure (AI replacements, layoffs) converting corporate workers into founders.
items.angle: If non-technical founders can build meaningful products using Claude + open models, the barrier to software creation has collapsed. This doesn't hurt incumbent software companies uniformly — it hurts the middleware and replaces the junior development layer.
items.tweetA: At SuperAI: wave of solopreneurs building AI products with no technical background. They take Claude subscriptions, workshops at Balaji's Network School, and vibe-code their ideas. Global layoffs are converting corporate workers into founders. The bottleneck is no longer 'can I code?' — it's 'do I have an idea?'
items.tweetB: The most interesting thing at SuperAI wasn't a model demo. It was the non-technical founders building actual products with Claude and Codex. No CS degree. No investors. Just an idea and access to frontier AI. The barrier to creating software has structurally collapsed.

items.title: Fintechs generating $650B in revenue; quantum at commercial tipping point with 300+ corporate adopters
items.idea: McKinsey Q2 2026 top reports: (1) Fintech sector generating $650B in revenue; top performers balancing scale, profitability, and regulatory maturity amid AI/digital assets transformation. (2) Quantum: 300+ global organizations actively collaborating with quantum technology companies — McKinsey calls this a 'commercial tipping point.' (3) B2B growth: divide between leaders and laggards driven by hyperpersonalization, AI integration. (4) AI meets luxury: brands must decide who controls how they are perceived in the agentic age.
items.angle: Fintech at $650B is a sector largely given up on by generalist investors who remember the 2022 reset. If the sector has rebuilt scale + profitability + regulatory standing, the re-rating trade could be real. McKinsey calling quantum a 'commercial tipping point' with 300+ adopters is also worth flagging.
items.tweetA: McKinsey Q2 2026: fintechs generating $650B in revenue. The sector that got crushed in 2022 has rebuilt scale, profitability, and regulatory maturity. Meanwhile: 300+ companies at the quantum computing commercial tipping point. Both of these feel under-priced relative to the coverage they're getting.
items.tweetB: McKinsey labels quantum computing a 'commercial tipping point.' 300+ global companies actively working with quantum vendors. Multibillion-dollar market already. This is not a 10-year bet anymore — it's a 2-3 year window. And most institutional allocators haven't moved.

items.title: Women's sports $2.5B opportunity; AI meets luxury — who controls brand perception in the agentic age
items.idea: McKinsey highlights a $2.5B opportunity in US women's sports — an inflection point with new leagues, growing audiences, and rising valuations. Infrastructure, strategy, and investment needed to realize the potential. Separately: in luxury, with consumers rapidly embracing AI, the strategic question for merchants is no longer whether agents will mediate the journey, but who controls how brands are perceived. The 'AI meets desire' framework for luxury brand strategy in an agentic world.
items.angle: The women's sports number is the actionable one: $2.5B in untapped opportunity suggests media rights, sponsorship, and infrastructure plays around women's leagues are still early innings. The luxury/AI angle is a second-order effect of AI agents increasingly mediating purchase decisions for high-value goods.
items.tweetA: McKinsey: US women's sports has hit an inflection point. $2.5B opportunity on the table. New leagues, rising valuations, growing audiences. The infrastructure, strategy, and investment to convert that into sustainable revenue is the question. Most of it hasn't happened yet.
items.tweetB: McKinsey's luxury + AI insight: the question for brands is no longer whether AI agents mediate the consumer journey. It's who controls how the brand is perceived when they do. This is the next brand strategy crisis — and most luxury houses haven't started thinking about it.
